questions.question: Explain the concept of 'this' pointer in C++ and state its type in a non-const member function.
keyPoints: The 'this' pointer is an implicit pointer that points to the current object invoking the member function
It holds the memory address of the current object instance
Type in non-const member function is 'ClassName *const' - a constant pointer to non-constant object
Available only in non-static member functions

questions.question: Why is the 'this' pointer useful for resolving name ambiguity between member variables and function parameters? Give an example.
keyPoints: When parameter names are same as member variable names, they shadow the member variables
Using this->member explicitly or implicitly (through compiler) distinguishes member from parameter
Example: void setX(int x) { this->x = x; } or { x = x; } with implicit this
Prevents ambiguity and ensures correct assignment to member variables

questions.question: How does function chaining work in C++ using the 'this' pointer? Illustrate with an example.
keyPoints: Function chaining is achieved by returning '*this' from member functions
Allows multiple method calls in a single statement like obj.setA(1).setB(2).display();
The returned object reference enables subsequent method calls on the same object
Example: Sample& setX(int x) { this->x = x; return *this; }

questions.question: What is the difference between 'this' and '*this' in C++?
keyPoints: 'this' is a pointer that holds the memory address of current object
'*this' dereferences the pointer, giving the actual object itself
'this' is used to access members via arrow operator (->)
'*this' is used to return the object by value or reference for chaining

questions.question: Explain why static member functions do not have access to the 'this' pointer.
keyPoints: Static member functions belong to the class, not to any specific object
Since they are not associated with any object, there's no 'this' pointer to provide
They can be called using ClassName::function() without creating an object
Static functions can access static members but not instance members requiring 'this'

questions.question: What happens to the type of 'this' pointer in a const member function? Explain with reason.
keyPoints: In const member functions, 'this' becomes 'const ClassName *const'
The const qualification makes the object read-only through 'this'
You cannot modify data members through 'this' in a const member function
This ensures const-correctness and prevents modification of object state

questions.question: Write a C++ class demonstrating the use of 'this' pointer in operator overloading for a binary operator.
keyPoints: Use 'this' to refer to the left operand object in binary operator
Return '*this' for compound assignment operators (like +=)
Example: Complex& operator+=(const Complex& c) { real += c.real; imag += c.imag; return *this; }
Enables chained operations and proper object-oriented semantics

questions.question: Explain the 'this' pointer in detail with its characteristics, uses, and importance in C++ object-oriented programming.
keyPoints: Definition: Implicit pointer pointing to current object instance
Key characteristics: constant pointer, type depends on const-qualification, passed as hidden argument
Uses: (1) Resolving name ambiguity, (2) Returning *this for chaining, (3) Operator overloading, (4) Passing object reference to other functions
Not available in static member functions
Importance: Enables proper object identity, method chaining, and self-reference in OOP
Type variations: ClassName*const in non-const functions, const ClassName*const in const functions

questions.question: Write a C++ program to demonstrate the use of 'this' pointer for method chaining. Create a class with setter methods that return *this and show a chained call.
keyPoints: Define a class (e.g., Calculator) with multiple setter/mutator methods
Each setter returns reference to *this: Calculator& setA(int val) { a = val; return *this; }
Demonstrate chaining: obj.setA(5).setB(10).setC(15);
Explain how this enables fluent interface pattern
Show complete program with constructor, display method, and main function

questions.question: Compare the 'this' pointer in const, non-const, and static member functions. Explain with code examples.
keyPoints: Non-const member function: this is ClassName*const - can modify object
Const member function: this is const ClassName*const - cannot modify object
Static member function: no this pointer exists - compiler error if used
Code examples showing each case with proper explanations
Practical implications for const-correctness and object behavior

questions.question: Design a C++ class demonstrating three practical uses of 'this' pointer: (i) name disambiguation, (ii) returning *this for chaining, (iii) operator overloading as left operand.
keyPoints: (i) Use this->member to distinguish from parameters with same name in setters
(ii) Return *this from setter methods to enable chaining like obj.setX(1).setY(2)
(iii) Implement operator+= using this as left operand: operator+=(const Obj& o) { members += o.members; return *this; }
Complete working program with all three features
Clear explanation of each use case and its benefits
